name: Co-marketing or integration partnership pitch
icp: Partnerships lead, head of marketing, or founder at an adjacent product
purpose: Open a conversation about a co-marketing or integration partnership
subjectLines: Customers asking about {{theirCompany}} on our side
body:
Hi {{firstName}},

{{specificObservation}}. We've had a handful of customers ask if we integrate with {{theirCompany}} — enough that I thought I'd reach out directly.

For context: {{yourCompany}} is {{oneLineDescription}}. Our overlap is {{overlapDescription}} — about {{overlapEstimate}} of our customer base also uses {{theirCompany}} or something like it.

Two concrete things I'd be interested in exploring, in order of effort:

1. {{lowEffortIdea}} — could ship in a week.
2. {{higherEffortIdea}} — bigger lift, but probably the more interesting one for both sides.

No deck, no contract — just a 25-min call to see if any of this is worth pursuing. Worst case, you get a sense of what our customers are asking for.

Who's the right person to talk to if it's not you?

{{senderFirstName}}

variables.note: Open with a real signal. e.g. 'Saw your launch on Product Hunt last week.'
What you do, in one sentence, no marketing language.
Where the customer bases meet. e.g. 'mid-market SaaS teams running outbound sales.'
A number you can defend. e.g. '40%'. If you don't actually know, drop this sentence.
Something concrete that takes <1 week. e.g. 'a joint webinar' or 'a product comparison page that cross-links.'
Something concrete that takes 1–3 months. e.g. 'a native integration that lets our users export segments to your tool.'

notes: Partnership pitches die in inboxes when they're vague about what's being proposed. The two-tier ask (low-effort + higher-effort) gives the recipient a way to say yes to something small. The 'who's the right person' close is intentional — partnerships people often forward these.
